pass_the_parcel.title: Ramp Launch: Upper-Mid Zone Placement
pass_the_parcel.principle: Place the red ball on the upper portion of the ramp at x ≈ ramp_center_x − 0.15 to −0.50 and y ≈ 3.8–4.2. This offset ensures the ball rolls down the ramp with sufficient leftward momentum to strike the top basket. The exact x-offset within this range modulates the angle of impact on the inverted basket.
pass_the_parcel.when_to_apply: Always use as the primary placement strategy; compute the x-offset from get_ramp_center.
pass_the_parcel.example: ramp_center_x=3.401 → try x=2.9; ramp_center_x=3.774 → try x=3.3

pass_the_parcel.title: y=4.2 as Preferred Default Launch Height
pass_the_parcel.principle: Setting y=4.2 for the red ball provides reliable impulse for both r=0.45 and r=0.5. Trajectories at y=4.0 frequently leave the green ball energy-deficient (stalling above the bottom basket), while y=4.2 consistently cleared this gap in successful runs. y=4.3 remains a fallback for cases with persistent energy deficit.
pass_the_parcel.when_to_apply: Use y=4.2 as the first-attempt height; verify y + r ≤ 4.95 to avoid ceiling clamp. Escalate to y=4.3 only if y=4.2 still leaves green ball stalled above the bottom basket.
pass_the_parcel.example: Seed 51: x=2.9, y=4.2, r=0.45 → SUCCESS after y=4.0 failed. Seed 53: x=3.3, y=4.2, r=0.5 → SUCCESS after y=4.0 failed.

pass_the_parcel.title: Safe Radius Range: r=0.45–0.52
pass_the_parcel.principle: Radii below 0.40 often produce insufficient impulse to dislodge the top basket. Radii above 0.52 frequently overshoot the green ball far left (x < −3.0), overshooting the bottom basket entirely. The r=0.45–0.52 range provides the best impulse-accuracy tradeoff.
pass_the_parcel.when_to_apply: Default to r=0.45 or r=0.5 on first attempt; do not exceed r=0.55 without overshoot analysis.
pass_the_parcel.example: Seed 52: r=0.6 → rim stall, r=0.7 → far overshoot to x=−4.73. r=0.5 is the safer choice.

pass_the_parcel.title: get_ramp_center Suggested Placement Trust
pass_the_parcel.principle: The get_ramp_center tool returns the ramp centroid, which is a reliable geometric anchor for computing red ball x-placement. Subtract 0.15–0.50 from ramp_center_x to land on the upper ramp surface. This x-offset calculation transfers correctly across different seed configurations.
pass_the_parcel.when_to_apply: Always call get_ramp_center before choosing x-placement; use it as the primary reference rather than hardcoded coordinates.

pass_the_parcel.title: Ramp Overlap Avoidance: Rightward x Boundary
pass_the_parcel.principle: The ramp is a solid diagonal bar. Placing the red ball too far right (x ≥ ramp_center_x − 0.05) causes the ball to collide with the ramp structure itself, producing an INVALID or misfired simulation. The rightward x boundary is approximately ramp_center_x − 0.10.
pass_the_parcel.when_to_apply: Before any simulate_action call, verify x < ramp_center_x − 0.10 to avoid ramp overlap.
pass_the_parcel.example: ramp_center_x=3.401 → keep x ≤ 3.30 to avoid overlap.

pass_the_parcel.title: Overshoot Detection: Binary Threshold Behavior
pass_the_parcel.principle: The level exhibits sharp non-monotonic thresholds: small changes in radius (Δ≈0.05–0.1) can flip the green ball from rim-stall to far-overshoot without passing through a success region. When r=0.5 produces rim stall and r=0.7 produces far overshoot, intermediate radii (r=0.6, 0.65) typically also produce rim stall — the threshold is extremely narrow. Switch to adjusting y instead of bisecting r.
pass_the_parcel.when_to_apply: When two radius values produce opposite failure modes (stall vs. overshoot), do not bisect. Instead hold r constant and increase y to add energy.
pass_the_parcel.example: Seed 52: r=0.5/0.6/0.65 → rim stall, r=0.7 → far overshoot. Bisecting r was futile; energy-axis adjustment needed.

pass_the_parcel.title: Basket-Fall Coupling: Velocity-Match Signals Active Re-Trapping
pass_the_parcel.principle: When the top basket and green ball share nearly identical velocities and positions during descent (green_y − basket_y < 0.6), the basket is re-trapping the green ball. A large x-shift alone may not break this coupling; increasing y (for more initial impulse) is often required to give the green ball enough velocity to separate from the basket during fall.
pass_the_parcel.when_to_apply: After any simulation where basket_y is within 0.6 of green_ball_y in the final state, increase y by 0.15–0.20 (not just x-shift).
pass_the_parcel.example: Seed 51: x=2.7, y=3.8 → basket at −2.311, green at −2.875 (gap 0.56) → re-trap. x=2.9, y=4.2 → separation achieved → SUCCESS.

pass_the_parcel.title: X-Bisection at Fixed Radius: Opposite-Direction Overshoots
pass_the_parcel.principle: When two x-values at the same radius produce green ball landings in opposite directions (one far left, one far right of the bottom basket), bisect x once between them. Apply only one bisection step before switching to a different parameter axis. Repeated bisection in this region is unreliable due to physics non-linearity.
pass_the_parcel.when_to_apply: When opposite-direction overshoots are confirmed at the same radius; bisect x once, then switch to y-axis if still failing.
pass_the_parcel.example: x=2.0 → green at x=+0.38 (undershoot/no dislodge), x=3.5 → green at x=−1.12 (overshoot). Try x=2.75 once, then adjust y.

pass_the_parcel.title: Ceiling-Bounded Y Placement for High Ramp Entries
pass_the_parcel.principle: The simulation enforces a hard ceiling at y=5.0; placements where y + radius > 5.0 are immediately rejected or clamped. For r=0.45, max safe y=4.55; for r=0.5, max safe y=4.50. Always verify y + r ≤ 4.95 before calling simulate_action to avoid wasted iterations.
pass_the_parcel.when_to_apply: Before every simulate_action call, check y + r ≤ 4.95.

pass_the_parcel.title: Low Ramp Position (ramp_y < 1.0) Creates Unsolvable Energy-Deficit Geometry
pass_the_parcel.principle: When the ramp center y-coordinate is below ~1.0, the ramp angle and position create a geometry where the launched red ball imparts insufficient downward momentum to the green ball regardless of x, y, or r adjustments within the safe ranges. In this configuration, the green ball consistently stalls on the bottom basket rim (y ≈ −3.0 to −3.7) with near-zero downward velocity across all parameter combinations.
pass_the_parcel.when_to_apply: At level start, check ramp center y from get_ramp_center. If ramp_y < 1.0, treat this as a high-difficulty case where standard ramp-launch strategy may be structurally insufficient. After 5+ failed attempts with systematic parameter variation, consider abandoning ramp launch and testing direct placement near the top basket.
pass_the_parcel.example: Seed 52: ramp_center=(3.707, 0.672) → 25 iterations all failed. Seeds 51/53 with ramp_y=2.28 and 1.70 succeeded in 5–6 iterations.

pass_the_parcel.description: Agent performs fine-grained bisection (radius OR y) in chaotic threshold regions where physics exhibits non-monotonic, direction-flipping behavior.
pass_the_parcel.why_it_happens: Agent treats the parameter space as smooth and continuous, assuming bisection will converge. In reality, threshold regions have discontinuous jumps.
pass_the_parcel.how_to_avoid: Bisect at most once between opposite failure modes, then switch to a different parameter axis entirely (e.g., from r to y).

pass_the_parcel.description: Agent fails to prevent the top basket from tracking alongside the green ball after dislodging, allowing it to re-trap or block the green ball mid-fall.
pass_the_parcel.why_it_happens: Agent focuses on x-coordinate adjustments when basket re-trapping occurs, not recognizing that more vertical energy (higher y) is needed to break velocity coupling.
pass_the_parcel.how_to_avoid: When final basket_y is within 0.6 of green_ball_y, increase y by 0.15–0.20 on the next attempt rather than shifting x alone.

pass_the_parcel.description: Agent ignores the simulation step budget as a hard physical constraint, repeatedly producing configurations where the green ball has the right direction but insufficient speed to reach the target within 2000 steps.
pass_the_parcel.why_it_happens: Agent interprets near-miss positions (green above blue) as close to success, when in fact the green ball has stopped moving with steps exhausted.
pass_the_parcel.how_to_avoid: Check final velocities — if green_ball velocity ≈ 0.0 at step 2000 with green still above blue, this is an energy-deficit failure requiring a y or r increase, not an x refinement.

pass_the_parcel.description: Agent repeatedly attempts placements that overlap with nearby objects (ramp surface, ceiling), producing INVALID simulations and wasting iterations.
pass_the_parcel.why_it_happens: Agent does not verify geometric constraints before each simulation call.
pass_the_parcel.how_to_avoid: Before every simulate_action: verify x < ramp_center_x − 0.10 and y + r ≤ 4.95.

pass_the_parcel.description: Agent continues parameter tuning when green ball stalls on the basket rim across all x/y/r variations, failing to recognize the structural ramp geometry incompatibility.
pass_the_parcel.why_it_happens: Agent assumes any failure is solvable by incremental adjustment. When ramp_y < 1.0, the geometry fundamentally limits energy transfer regardless of parameter choice.
pass_the_parcel.how_to_avoid: If green ball stalls on the rim (y ≈ −3.0 to −3.7, velocity ≈ 0) across 5+ varied attempts spanning x ∈ [2.0, 3.5], y ∈ [3.8, 4.45], r ∈ [0.45, 0.7], recognize this as a structural failure and attempt direct placement near the top basket instead of continued ramp-launch tuning.
